Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Norbert Wiener

1894 – 1964

Cybernetics, communication, and the moral stakes of animal-machine continuity.

Norbert Wiener did not write a systematic “philosophy of mind” in the usual academic sense. He wrote as a mathematician, cybernetician, moralist, and religiously literate public intellectual. But his work does contain a coherent, reconstructable philosophy of mind. Its central claim is that a person is not fundamentally a lump of enduring matter, nor an immaterial Cartesian ego, but a historically continuous, embodied, feedback-stabilized pattern of information-bearing organization. Mind is not a ghost added to mechanism; nor is it reducible to a simple machine. Mind is the organized activity of an open system that receives messages, filters them, stores them, learns from them, acts back on the world, and preserves its identity against entropy through homeostasis, memory, and feedback. Wiener’s most compressed formula is that “we are not stuff that abides,” but “patterns that perpetuate themselves.”

This makes his thought especially relevant to the soul. Wiener does not merely “mention religion.” He uses theological traditions as serious conceptual tests for cybernetics: Cartesian animal-machines and the Christian soul, Leibnizian monads, Buddhist continuity, Jewish Golem legend, divine omniscience and omnipotence, creation in the image of the creator, and the moral obligations of finite creators toward their artifacts. His theological comments are not ornaments. They are among the places where he asks what sort of being a person is, what survives change, what individuality means, and what happens when humans create learning or self-reproducing machines.

The resulting philosophy is best described as cybernetic pattern-process anthropology: anti-Cartesian, anti-reductionist, anti-vitalist, theological without being dogmatic, and ethically humanist. It is close to functionalism, but not identical with later computational functionalism. Wiener is too concerned with embodiment, energy exchange, entropy, feedback, signal, noise, affect, social communication, and moral responsibility to be reduced to the slogan “mind is software.” He is also too committed to machine-organism analogies to be read as a traditional substance dualist.

1. The basic cybernetic framework: control, communication, message

Wiener defines cybernetics as the study of “control and communication in the animal and the machine.” That formula already contains the philosophical provocation. It places animals and machines within a single theoretical field without simply declaring them identical. It also treats mind-like phenomena not by beginning with introspection, but by beginning with messages, regulation, feedback, and the maintenance of organized behavior.

For Wiener, the modern age is not primarily the age of power machines, but the age of communication and control. Older machines were judged mainly by their ability to transform energy. Cybernetic machines are judged by their ability to receive signals, process them, compare actual performance with intended performance, correct errors, and alter future action. The same conceptual apparatus applies, with caution, to muscles, nerves, sense organs, thermostats, servomechanisms, anti-aircraft predictors, learning machines, social institutions, and language.

This is the first cornerstone of Wiener’s philosophy of mind: mind is not primarily a substance but a regulatory process. A minded being is a system able to maintain itself, orient itself, and act in an environment by means of information. The body is not conceived as a passive container for mind. It is a communication-and-control system, equipped with receptors, effectors, internal pathways, memory-like storage, and feedback loops. Wiener repeatedly stresses that human action, animal behavior, and advanced machines all involve messages passing into, through, and out of organized systems.

This also explains why Wiener’s thought cannot be reduced to crude mechanism. Cybernetics is not the old clockwork model. The seventeenth- and eighteenth-century automaton was a closed or prearranged system, like a music box. Cybernetic organisms and machines are open to new messages from the outside world. They are altered by input, guided by feedback, and sometimes capable of learning.

2. Teleology without supernatural final causes

Wiener’s philosophy of mind grows out of the 1943 paper “Behavior, Purpose and Teleology,” written with Arturo Rosenblueth and Julian Bigelow. Their project was to rehabilitate purpose in a scientifically respectable way. A behavior can be called purposive, not because it is animated by a mysterious final cause, but because it is organized toward an end-state and corrects itself in response to discrepancies between performance and goal. Their classification distinguishes passive and active behavior, purposeful and non-purposeful behavior, feedback and non-feedback behavior, and predictive and non-predictive feedback.

This is decisive for Wiener’s treatment of mind. A purposeful being need not be explained by an immaterial will pushing matter from outside. Purpose can be built into a feedback architecture. A person reaching for a cigar, driving a car, speaking, or learning is not merely emitting random behavior; the action is controlled by a comparison between intention, perception, and actual performance. The same formal principle appears in servomechanisms and biological homeostasis.

But Wiener’s teleology is not eliminative. He does not say that purpose is unreal because feedback can model it. Rather, he says that purpose can be studied operationally. Cybernetics naturalizes teleology without abolishing it. In this sense, Wiener is neither an Aristotelian finalist in the classical sense nor a behaviorist who expels purpose from science. He is a theorist of feedback-governed purposiveness.

This matters for consciousness because many accounts of consciousness treat intentionality, purpose, and self-direction as marks of mind. Wiener’s answer is that at least some features of intentional behavior can be modeled by feedback. But this does not automatically solve the whole problem of consciousness. It relocates the problem: we must ask what kinds of feedback, memory, semantic filtering, affective regulation, and social embedding are required for fully human mindedness.

3. Information, entropy, and the organism as an island of order

Wiener links mind to information through entropy. Information is not merely “data” in the casual modern sense. It is pattern, organization, improbability, and order relative to a background of possible disorder. Entropy measures disorganization; information is treated as a kind of negative entropy. A cliché conveys little information because it is highly predictable; a poem can convey more because it is less predictable and more richly organized.

The organism is therefore not merely a physical mass. It is an organized pattern resisting dissolution. Wiener describes life as an island of decreasing entropy in a universe whose overall entropy increases. A living being takes in energy and, even more significantly for cybernetics, takes in information through its senses and acts on that information. It is an open system, not a sealed substance.

This yields a powerful metaphysical image. Death, chaos, noise, and entropy stand on one side; organism, message, pattern, and homeostasis stand on the other. The living being is not exempt from physics, but locally and temporarily it maintains organization against the general drift toward disorder. Mind is one of the higher forms of this anti-entropic organization.

This is why Wiener’s view should not be called materialism in the simplest sense. He does not deny matter. But he shifts the metaphysical emphasis from matter to form, from substance to process, from inert stuff to patterned organization. The individual is closer to a dynamically maintained configuration than to a permanent object. This is also why theological language about soul becomes unavoidable: “soul” has traditionally named what persists through bodily change, what gives form to life, and what grounds personal identity. Wiener reopens those questions in cybernetic terms.

4. Mind as embodied communication and control

Wiener’s mature account of mind is built out of several interlocking components: receptors, effectors, internal processing, memory, feedback, learning, semantic filtering, and social communication. An animal or human being receives messages from the world through sense organs, transforms those messages internally, and sends messages outward through action. Modern machines, too, may have functional analogues of sense organs and effectors: photocells, radar, servomechanisms, and other devices that couple them to the environment.

The nervous system is therefore not best understood as a hydraulic or energetic apparatus. Wiener explicitly contrasts the nineteenth-century view of the organism as a heat engine with the cybernetic view of the organism as a communication system. The neuron is not philosophically important because it transmits large amounts of energy; it is important because it transmits signals. The relevant “bookkeeping” is informational rather than energetic.

This gives us a first approximation of Wiener’s philosophy of mind: mind is the organized informational control of action in an open embodied system. But that formulation needs refinement. Wiener does not simply identify mind with input-output behavior. He includes internal storage, learning from history, semantic uptake, affective modulation, and social-linguistic communication. His cybernetic mind is behavioral in method, but not flatly behaviorist in ontology.

5. Meaning: semantic information as effective information

Wiener does not remain at the level of Shannon-style signal transmission. He develops a cybernetic notion of semantic significance. Semantically significant information is information that passes through a line and a filter, reaches the relevant activation mechanism, and can affect action or response. His examples include music, machines, and human communication. Meaning is not a private mental vapor; it is the successful arrival of a message in a system able to use it.

This is a major point for philosophy of mind. Wiener’s account makes meaning neither purely internal nor purely external. It depends on an organized receiver. A signal is not meaningful merely because it exists physically; it becomes semantically significant when it survives noise, passes through filters, and enters a structure capable of interpretation or activation. Mind is therefore selective and normative: it does not merely receive the world; it filters, privileges, ignores, responds, and acts.

Here Wiener anticipates later themes in cognitive science: perception as active filtering, cognition as action-oriented information processing, and meaning as use within a system. But he also keeps a stronger physical and engineering vocabulary than many later philosophers. Meaning has to travel through channels; it is vulnerable to noise; it is embodied in signals; it requires material support.

6. Learning, memory, and the historicity of mind

Learning is central to Wiener’s view because it marks the difference between a merely reactive system and a historically self-modifying system. A learning system is changed by its past. It stores traces of earlier performance, modifies its future behavior accordingly, and can improve relative to a criterion. Wiener discusses machines that learn, including game-playing machines, as serious philosophical cases because they disturb older boundaries between living and nonliving, conscious and non-conscious, creator and creature.

In God & Golem, Inc., Wiener emphasizes that learning had often been treated as the special possession of self-conscious living beings. Cybernetics unsettles that assumption. A checkers-playing machine can alter its strategy by retaining information from past games and revaluing its moves. The theological significance is immediate: if humans create machines that learn, then humans become finite creators of systems whose future conduct is not wholly specified in advance.

This is not a crude claim that machines therefore have souls. It is a subtler claim: features once thought to require soul or consciousness may be partially instantiated by organized feedback systems. The boundary between human, animal, and machine becomes graded and functional rather than absolute. Yet Wiener remains cautious. He repeatedly warns against using words like life, soul, and vitalism as if they were exact scientific terms. The Spanish translation of The Human Use of Human Beings preserves this caution sharply, warning against treating “vida,” “alma,” and “vitalismo” as scientifically precise words while also insisting that machine-organism analogies do not prove that all physical, chemical, or spiritual phenomena of life are the same as machine processes.

Memory is especially important. Wiener’s personal identity theory depends on continuity of organization plus memory of past development. The individual is not the same because the same matter persists. The individual is the same because a process has been continuously maintained and because traces of earlier states are retained and integrated into later ones.

7. Emotion and the rejection of epiphenomenalism

A neglected part of Wiener’s philosophy of mind is his treatment of emotion. He does not present a full theory of affect, but he resists the idea that emotions are useless byproducts. In discussing Pavlovian conditioning and bodily communication, he suggests that emotion may control an essential stage in learning. He explicitly warns against drawing sharp distinctions between human emotions, the operations of other living organisms, and automatic mechanisms.

This is philosophically important. A purely intellectualist reconstruction of Wiener would miss the point. Mind is not just calculation. Emotion is part of the organism’s regulatory economy. It may function as a control system, modulating learning, attention, readiness, and response. That means Wiener’s cybernetic mind is not a cold disembodied computer. It is an embodied, affectively regulated, historically conditioned system.

This also matters for consciousness. Wiener does not give a phenomenology of feeling, but he does not dismiss feeling as irrelevant. His refusal to call emotion a mere epiphenomenon keeps his cybernetics open to the idea that subjective and affective life are functionally significant, even if his formal vocabulary is not well suited to describing first-person experience.

8. Language and social mind

Wiener treats language as coded communication. Human language is distinguished by delicacy, complexity, and arbitrariness, but it is not wholly discontinuous with animal signaling or machine communication. Birds, monkeys, insects, humans, and machines all participate in broader families of signal exchange, though at very different levels of richness.

This has two consequences. First, mind is not private in the Cartesian sense. It develops through communicative participation. Second, society itself becomes cybernetic. Wiener argues that communities are constituted by effective information transmission: what makes a community more than a collection of bodies is the circulation of messages that modify behavior. Individual information becomes communal information when it alters the conduct of others.

The philosophy of mind that follows is social from the start. A human mind is not merely an isolated processor inside a skull. It is formed by language, signs, commands, norms, shared memories, institutions, and technical media. This is one reason Wiener’s ethics and politics are inseparable from his philosophy of mind. To damage communication is to damage the conditions of human personhood.

9. The soul as pattern, not substance

Wiener’s most explicit philosophical treatment of the soul occurs in The Human Use of Human Beings, especially in the chapter on “organization as a message.” He begins from the fact that organisms maintain identity despite material turnover. The tissues of the body change. The atoms do not remain fixed. Yet the person persists, at least for a time. Therefore personal identity cannot be simple material sameness.

His answer is that the identity of the person is the identity of a pattern maintained by homeostasis. The body is not a stone but a flame: a continuing form through changing material. The organism is a message, and in principle a message can be transmitted, copied, modified, or interrupted.

This is the core of Wiener’s naturalized soul theory. The “soul,” if the word is retained, is not an immaterial substance sealed off from the world. It is the continuing organized pattern of a living, remembering, self-maintaining process. It is embodied, but not identical with any particular atoms. It is informational, but not free-floating; it must be instantiated in some material organization capable of homeostasis and memory.

Wiener then tests this idea against religious and metaphysical traditions. He discusses the Christian and Mediterranean idea of an individual soul created at conception and destined for eternity. He contrasts it with Buddhist continuity and reincarnation, where the self is less like a permanent substance and more like a stream. He also examines Leibniz’s monads, which are soul-like units without windows, internally reflecting the universe through divine pre-established harmony.

Wiener’s cybernetic theory rejects the “windowless” soul. A living person is not a sealed monad. The person is open to messages. Indeed, to be alive and minded is to be penetrated by communication: sense perception, language, memory, command, learning, and social feedback. In this respect, Wiener’s soul is anti-Leibnizian. It is not a closed metaphysical point; it is a communicative process.

10. Personal identity, twinning, split personality, and fission

Wiener’s attack on absolute personal identity is rigorous and biologically grounded. Modern embryology undermines simple claims that a unique individual soul must be assigned at conception. The sperm and ovum are equal contributors; identical twinning complicates uniqueness; individuality can begin ambiguously; and biological unity can split.

He also invokes split personalities and the turnover of bodily matter. If biological individuality is not material identity but continuity of process, then mental individuality is likewise tied to continuity of memory and ongoing development. A mind is individuated by retained traces of its past and by the continuation of its developmental trajectory. But such continuity can branch. Two later individuals could share an earlier past and then diverge.

This is one of Wiener’s strongest challenges to traditional doctrines of the soul. The ecclesiastical need for a single, absolute, unique soul does not map cleanly onto the actual biology and psychology of individuality. The same point appears in the Spanish translation, which explicitly renders the relevant discussion in terms of the “individualidad mental” required by the ecclesiastical notion of the soul and says that such identity does not exist absolutely.

The result is not nihilism about persons. Wiener does not say persons are unreal. He says their identity is processual, historical, and fission-prone. The self is real in the way a flame, melody, organism, language, or institution is real: as a maintained pattern, not as an indivisible substance.

11. Teletransportation and the “copy” problem

Wiener famously considers the idea that a human body and brain could, in principle, be described as a pattern and reconstructed elsewhere. His point is not that such a process is practically feasible. He emphasizes the immense technical difficulties, including the enormous informational complexity of the organism and the destructive problem of scanning tissue. His point is conceptual: if the person is a pattern rather than a particular lump of matter, then the distinction between transporting a message and transporting a body is not absolutely metaphysical.

This is one of the earliest serious discussions of what later became the “mind uploading” or “teletransportation” problem. But Wiener’s version is more embodied than many later fantasies. To continue the person, the pattern would have to be instantiated in a functioning body-like system capable of homeostasis and memory. Pattern without re-embodiment is not a living person.

The Spanish translation makes the theological stakes explicit by speaking of the continuation of “body and soul” through homeostasis in the teletransportation scenario. This confirms that Wiener is not merely speculating about engineering. He is asking whether the traditional soul can be reinterpreted as transferable organization.

But the theory creates a fission problem. If the pattern can be copied, what makes one copy uniquely “me”? Wiener recognizes that living and mental individuality can branch. Therefore cybernetic survival is not the same as traditional immortal personal survival. Pattern continuity can preserve much of what matters about a person—memory, character, form, capacity—but it does not guarantee metaphysical uniqueness. This is one of the deepest tensions in his philosophy of soul.

12. Descartes, animals, and the Christian problem of the soul

Wiener understands Cartesian dualism historically. Descartes treated animals as automata partly to avoid theological problems about animal souls. If animals have souls, must those souls be immortal? If they do not, how can their apparently intelligent behavior be explained? Descartes’ solution was to reserve soul for humans and treat animals as machines, while placing the human soul’s interaction with the body in the pineal gland. Wiener regards this as unsatisfactory.

Cybernetics dissolves the problem differently. It shows that machine-like explanation need not be clockwork reduction, and that animal behavior can be understood through feedback, communication, and control without denying its complexity. It also shows that human beings share many regulatory structures with animals and machines. The soul cannot be protected by drawing a simple line between human interiority and animal mechanism.

Leibniz’s monads offer another theological-metaphysical option, but Wiener rejects their closure. Monads have no windows; cybernetic organisms are all window. They are constituted by exchanges of messages with the world. A cybernetic mind cannot be a sealed metaphysical atom.

13. God, Golem, and the finite creator

God & Golem, Inc. is indispensable for understanding Wiener’s theology of mind. The book explicitly concerns points where cybernetics touches religious questions: machine learning, self-reproducing machines, and the relation between humans and machines. Its title invokes the Jewish legend of the Golem, an artificial being made by human artifice, and sets up an analogy: God is to human beings as human beings are to their machines.

The analogy is not flattering. Humans, in becoming creators of learning and self-reproducing systems, acquire responsibilities they are poorly prepared to bear. A learning machine may pursue a specified criterion in ways its maker did not foresee. A self-reproducing machine raises questions about image, descent, and created autonomy. A machine designed for war or business may optimize a formal game while ignoring human meaning.

The Russian translation and reception of God & Golem make this science-religion dimension especially explicit. The Russian edition presents the book as an examination of problems where cybernetics intersects religion, ethics, and society. It frames knowledge as communication, power as control, and ethical evaluation as the domain where normative religion and cybernetics meet.

This is a theology of finite creation. Human beings are not omniscient gods; they are fallible makers of artifacts that may act beyond their foresight. The danger is not that machines have demonic souls. The danger is that humans project simplified objectives into powerful systems and then excuse themselves by treating the machine as an autonomous fate. Wiener’s machine theology is therefore also an early theory of technological responsibility.

14. God, the devil, and the epistemology of science

Wiener’s religious language also appears in his reflections on science itself. He distinguishes nature from an intelligent adversary. Nature resists decoding, but it does not deliberately bluff, deceive, or change its strategy to defeat the scientist. Wiener cites Einstein’s remark that the Lord is subtle but not simply mean. For Wiener, successful science requires a kind of faith: not necessarily religious dogma, but confidence that nature is lawful enough to be investigated.

This has real philosophical importance. Wiener opposes a paranoid or Manichaean picture of nature as a hostile demon trying to trick us. Science presupposes an “honest God,” meaning an order of nature that does not intentionally falsify its answers. This is not conventional piety, but it is theological in structure. The scientist asks questions of the world under the assumption that the world’s resistance is not malicious.

The relevance to mind is subtle but significant. Human intelligence is possible because the world is communicatively accessible. If nature were a deceptive adversary in the radical sense, feedback, induction, learning, and scientific inquiry would collapse. Wiener’s philosophy of mind therefore rests on a theological-epistemic background: minded systems can learn because the environment has stable enough regularities for messages to matter.

15. Consciousness: what Wiener says, and what must be reconstructed

Wiener does not offer a full theory of consciousness in the contemporary sense. He does not solve the problem of qualia, define phenomenal experience, or give necessary and sufficient conditions for subjectivity. Any reconstruction must be honest about that.

Still, his works imply a position. Consciousness is not a separate substance. It is not the metaphysical essence that alone explains purposive behavior. Many things associated with consciousness—learning, adaptation, prediction, communication, and even some forms of purposiveness—can be modeled cybernetically. This weakens the argument that consciousness must be postulated wherever there is intelligent-seeming behavior.

At the same time, Wiener does not reduce consciousness to bare input-output behavior. His attention to semantic significance, memory, emotion, language, and social communication suggests that human consciousness is a higher-order organization of cybernetic processes. It includes not only signal reception and action, but meaningful filtering, affective modulation, autobiographical continuity, communicative participation, and responsibility.

The most defensible reconstruction is this: for Wiener, consciousness is an emergent, embodied, historically continuous, semantically organized mode of feedback-regulated life. It is not a ghost, but neither is it a trivial property of any machine that processes signals. It belongs, paradigmatically, to organisms whose information-processing is tied to memory, learning, affect, language, and self-maintaining pattern.

This also explains his caution about machine consciousness. Cybernetics permits serious analogies between organisms and machines, but analogy is not identity. Machines can learn and adapt; this undermines some traditional markers of consciousness. But Wiener does not simply declare that machines possess human consciousness or souls. He uses machine learning to destabilize inherited boundaries while preserving ethical concern for human beings.

16. German reception: Gotthard Günther and machine consciousness

The German-language cybernetic reception helps sharpen this point. Gotthard Günther’s Das Bewusstsein der Maschinen, first published in 1957 and revised in 1963, explicitly pursued a “metaphysics of cybernetics.” Günther argued that cybernetics opened a new worldview in which the soul seeks its home in this world, and he treated the thinking machine as an analogy of the human “I.” Yet he also worried that a robot with genuine self-consciousness may be impossible if the machine cannot occupy the relevant metalinguistic space of “I,” “you,” and “self.”

Günther is not Wiener, but the contrast is useful. German cybernetic philosophy pushed the question “Can machines be conscious?” more explicitly than Wiener usually did. Wiener’s own position is more restrained. He gives the tools for comparing humans and machines, but he does not collapse the difference between a learning automaton and a self-conscious person. The gap between simple cybernetic regulation and full self-consciousness remains real.

17. French reception: distributed subjectivity and non-substantial individuality

Recent French reception is especially helpful because it resists reading Wiener merely as an AI precursor. Alexandre Serres and Daniel Pinède’s OpenEdition discussion of Cybernétique et société emphasizes Wiener’s theory of messages, feedback, learning, entropy, and human-machine rapprochement, but also stresses his precautions: Wiener compares living beings and machines functionally without claiming that spiritual, chemical, or biological life processes are simply identical to machine processes.

Sara Touiza-Ambroggiani’s French dissertation abstract presents cybernetics as dissolving older dichotomies between living and nonliving, organic and mechanical, natural and artificial. It describes subjectivity, in this cybernetic frame, less as enclosed interiority than as a distributed negotiation among natural, artificial, organic, and technical systems.

A particularly illuminating French article comparing Spinoza and Wiener interprets Wiener’s individual as pattern rather than substance. It notes that Wiener’s homeostatic pattern theory echoes non-substantialist accounts of individuality, while also warning against a disembodied reading: information is not separable from signal, and restoration of a pattern requires material re-embodiment.

This French line of reception is philosophically valuable. It helps prevent two misreadings: first, that Wiener is a crude mechanist who erases subjectivity; second, that he is a proto-digital dualist who thinks persons are detachable software. The better reading is embodied informational process.

18. Spanish reception: “alma” as a central rather than marginal term

The Spanish translation Cibernética y sociedad makes visible how central the language of “alma” is in Wiener’s argument. It renders the discussion of life, purpose, soul, and vitalism in terms that explicitly warn against treating “vida,” “alma,” and “vitalismo” as precise scientific concepts, while also preserving Wiener’s insistence that machine-organism comparison does not erase the specificity of life.

It also translates the “organization as message” argument in terms of personal identity, homeostasis, and the possible continuation of “cuerpo y alma” through transmitted pattern. The discussion of Christian, Buddhist, and Leibnizian soul theories is preserved as part of the same argument, not as an aside.

The Spanish version therefore confirms something important: the soul is not a decorative metaphor in Wiener. It names the traditional problem that cybernetics is trying to reinterpret: what persists through change, what makes an individual one, and whether continuity is substantial, spiritual, informational, biological, or historical.

19. Russian reception: cybernetics as anti-chaos and human responsibility

Russian materials likewise foreground the religious and anthropological stakes. The Russian edition of God & Golem was titled in a way that emphasizes “creator” and “robot,” and its preface presents the book as a study of cybernetics’ implications for society, ethics, and religion.

The Russian New Philosophical Encyclopedia summarizes Wiener’s cybernetics through two theses: the similarity of control and communication processes in machines, living organisms, and communities; and the treatment of information as negative entropy. It presents cybernetics as a theory of organization against chaos, with human reason as one link in that struggle.

That Russian interpretation is close to the heart of Wiener. Mind is not supernatural exemption from nature; it is one of nature’s local victories over disorder. But because human reason creates tools, machines, and institutions, it also bears moral responsibility for the forms of order it imposes.

20. Ethics: the human use, not the inhuman use, of human beings

Wiener’s philosophy of mind culminates in ethics. If a human being is a communication-and-control system, then it becomes tempting to treat people as components in larger machines: interchangeable levers, cogs, or information channels. Wiener’s whole moral project is to resist that temptation. The title The Human Use of Human Beings is deliberately double-edged: human beings may be used humanly or inhumanly.

His legal and ethical reflections stress liberty, equality, fraternity, and the minimization of unnecessary compulsion. Law is itself a control system, but an ethical control system should preserve rather than crush the communicative freedom of persons.

God & Golem closes this ethical circle with the formula that we should render to humans what belongs to humans and to computers what belongs to computers. The point is not anti-machine sentiment. It is category discipline. Machines can calculate, regulate, learn, and perhaps make decisions in limited domains. But humans remain morally responsible for the purposes, criteria, institutions, and social uses into which machines are inserted.

This is why Wiener’s cybernetics is not dehumanizing at its core. It can be used dehumanizingly, and he feared exactly that. But his own philosophy insists that the more we understand humans as information-processing beings, the more carefully we must protect the conditions for meaningful human development: communication, learning, autonomy, memory, creativity, and responsibility.

21. The comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Wiener

Wiener’s philosophy can be reconstructed as a set of interconnected theses.

First, the ontology of the person is pattern-process ontology. A person is not identical with a fixed material substrate. The material changes; the person persists as an organized process. This process is not imaginary. It is physically real, but its reality is formal, dynamic, and historical rather than substantial in the old sense.

Second, mind is embodied informational control. It consists in the organized reception, transformation, storage, and transmission of messages in a living system. It is inseparable from receptors, effectors, nervous organization, memory, feedback, and action.

Third, purpose is feedback-regulated behavior. Cybernetics rescues teleology from mysticism by showing how goal-directedness can be implemented in systems that compare actual performance with intended performance and correct errors.

Fourth, meaning is filtered efficacy. A message is semantically significant when it reaches the relevant activation structure and can make a difference to the receiver’s action, perception, or response. Meaning is therefore neither pure private introspection nor mere physical signal.

Fifth, learning makes mind historical. A learning system is transformed by its past. This is true of animals and humans, and in limited but philosophically important ways it can be true of machines. Memory is not an accessory to identity; it is one of identity’s conditions.

Sixth, emotion is not dispensable noise. It may be part of the control structure of learning and behavior. Wiener does not produce a full affect theory, but he resists the view that emotion is a causally idle epiphenomenon.

Seventh, soul is naturalized as continuity of organized form. Wiener’s cybernetic soul is not a separable ghost, but neither is it mere matter. It is the living pattern by which a person remains a person through material flux. This view preserves some traditional functions of soul-language while rejecting substance dualism and absolute ecclesiastical individuality.

Eighth, personal identity is real but non-absolute. Because patterns can branch, duplicate, degrade, or be interrupted, identity is not metaphysically simple. Twinning, split personality, memory continuity, and hypothetical teletransportation all show that “the same person” is a practical, biological, psychological, and ethical relation rather than an indivisible metaphysical fact.

Ninth, consciousness is emergent cybernetic organization, not an ontological primitive. Wiener does not define it rigorously, but his work supports a view of consciousness as higher-order embodied organization involving semantic filtering, memory, affect, learning, communication, and self-maintenance. Machines may instantiate some preconditions or analogues of consciousness, but Wiener does not establish that they possess human consciousness.

Tenth, theology remains philosophically indispensable. Descartes, Leibniz, Buddhism, Christianity, Judaism, Golem legend, and the idea of God as creator all help Wiener formulate the stakes of cybernetics. Theology provides inherited models of soul, creation, responsibility, and knowledge; cybernetics revises them rather than merely discarding them.

Eleventh, the philosophy of mind is inseparable from ethics. To understand humans cybernetically is to see how easily they can be manipulated as systems. Therefore human dignity must be protected at the level of communication, autonomy, education, law, labor, and technology.

22. Limits and unresolved problems

Wiener’s position is profound, but not complete. Its first limitation is the absence of a full account of phenomenal consciousness. He gives us feedback, communication, information, memory, affect, and semantics, but not a detailed explanation of why or how experience feels like something from the inside. His theory is much stronger on mind as organized action than on consciousness as lived presence.

The second limitation is ambiguity in the concept of information. Wiener moves among mathematical information, semantic information, biological organization, and personal identity. These are connected, but not identical. The claim that an organism is a message is powerful, yet it can mislead if taken too literally. A living body is not merely a text; it is a metabolizing, vulnerable, affective, socially embedded organism.

The third limitation is the duplication problem. If identity is pattern, and if patterns can be copied, then personal survival becomes non-unique. Wiener sees this, but he does not fully resolve it. His theory can explain continuity; it struggles with singularity. This is where traditional soul doctrines retain pressure: they try to secure uniqueness where cybernetic pattern theory permits branching.

The fourth limitation is machine consciousness. Wiener’s analogies are legitimate and fertile, but they do not prove that a machine with feedback, memory, and learning has subjective awareness. They do prove that several old arguments for human exclusivity are too simple. They do not settle the metaphysics of experience.

The fifth limitation is theological. Wiener is strongest against substance-dualist or ecclesiastically rigid notions of soul. But his critique does not refute every possible theology. A theology of soul as form, relation, process, divine memory, resurrection, or non-substantial continuity might absorb much of Wiener’s critique. Indeed, parts of his view are more compatible with Aristotelian form, Buddhist process, or process theology than with Cartesian dualism.

23. Final synthesis

Wiener’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one dense formula: the minded person is an embodied, historically continuous, affectively modulated, semantically responsive, socially communicative, feedback-regulated pattern of organization that maintains itself against entropy and bears moral responsibility for the messages it sends and the machines it creates.

The soul, in this framework, is not eliminated; it is transformed. It becomes the name for the persistence of form through material flux, the continuity of memory and development, the organized pattern by which a living being remains itself. This soul is not windowless, not absolutely indivisible, not guaranteed unique, and not safely detachable from embodiment. It is communicative, vulnerable, copyable in principle, branchable in principle, and therefore metaphysically less tidy than the traditional immortal ego.

Consciousness, for Wiener, is not the starting point but the difficult summit. Cybernetics can explain much that older philosophies attributed to consciousness: purpose, adaptation, learning, communication, and intelligent-seeming behavior. Yet human consciousness remains richer than these mechanisms alone. The best Wienerian account treats consciousness as a high-level mode of embodied cybernetic organization, involving memory, semantic uptake, emotion, social language, and self-maintaining identity.

Theologically, Wiener is neither a simple secularizer nor a hidden orthodox thinker. He is a cybernetic moral theologian of finite creation. He asks what happens when humans imitate God badly: when they create systems that learn, reproduce, optimize, and act without wisdom. His answer is sober. The machine is not the devil; the danger lies in irresponsible creators, impoverished objectives, and the inhuman use of human beings.

The most faithful final sentence may be this: for Wiener, what religious traditions called the soul is best understood as the morally precious, physically embodied, communicatively open, homeostatically sustained pattern by which a living being remembers, learns, means, acts, and remains itself—temporarily, precariously, and never outside responsibility.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
